name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: Gamifying day-trading, payment for order flow, and encouraging options trading prevent Robinhood from being a fiduciary to its customers, as required by Massachusetts law. Robinhood needs to make changes to protect its’ customers best interests.

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: Due to the complexity of options trading, new or casual investors are destined to lose out to experienced options traders.   68% of MA customers approved for options trading on @RobinhoodApp had limited to no investment experience.

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: Options are a complicated investment tool. Yes, they are a fast way to make money. They are also a fast way to lose money. Industry regulators require brokerages to vet customers who want to trade options. Robinhood, by contrast, pushes options onto its users.

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: Payment for order flow is Robinhood’s primary source of revenue and the reason it can offer commission-free trading to customers.   Doesn’t payment for order flow incentivize Robinhood to encourage customers to trade as often as possible regardless of their trade quality?

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: Robinhood must explain how it can claim to comply with Massachusetts’ best-interest rule when it clearly incentivizes everyday people to become day traders.

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: The vast majority of retail day traders, who try to pick individual stocks in competition with professional traders, lose money – and only about 1% consistently get better returns than low-cost index funds.

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
tweet: Right now, half of US households have no stocks at all. Democratizing access to assets is an important objective – we *want* Main Street to own more of Wall Street. However, the best way to do that is through low-cost index funds, mutual funds, or ETFs.

name: Rep. Jake Auchincloss
